title: The man who looks inside old books
summary: In a small Paris bookshop, a quiet man checks every used book for forgotten letters — and keeps them in a wooden box.
body:
There is a small bookshop on Rue de Seine in Paris. The owner is Arthur. He is sixty-eight. He sells used books.

Every morning, Arthur opens each new book. He opens it slowly, page by page. He is not reading. He is looking.

He is looking for letters.

People use letters as bookmarks. They forget them. A letter from a mother. A letter from a friend. A short note on a small piece of paper. Sometimes a photo. Arthur finds them all.

He puts each letter in a wooden box behind the counter. The box is full. He has done this for thirty years.

'Why do you keep them?' a young woman asked him one day. She was a tourist.

Arthur said, 'They are not mine. But they are not nothing. Somebody wrote them. Somebody put them in a book. I cannot throw them away. So I keep them.'

'Do people come back for them?' she asked.

'Almost never,' he said. 'Two times in thirty years. A man came for his father's letter. A woman came for a photo of her sister.'

'And the rest?'

'They wait,' Arthur said. 'The box waits. I wait too.'

The tourist bought a small book. She did not put a letter inside. But she thought about Arthur for many years.

annotations.meaningVi: sách cũ / sách đã qua sử dụng
vật để đánh dấu trang sách
vứt chúng đi (phrasal verb: throw away)

grammarNotes.title: Present simple for daily habits
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Bài dùng hiện tại đơn để tả thói quen hằng ngày của Arthur: 'Every morning, Arthur opens each new book.' 'He is not reading. He is looking.' Khi muốn nói một việc lặp đi lặp lại, ta dùng động từ ở dạng nguyên (với 'he/she/it' thêm -s). Khi muốn nhấn việc đang xảy ra ngay lúc đó, ta dùng 'is + V-ing'.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: The passage uses present simple for Arthur's daily habits: 'Every morning, Arthur opens each new book.' Contrast with present continuous: 'He is not reading. He is looking.' Present simple = repeated habit; present continuous = action happening right now.

translationVi:
Có một hiệu sách nhỏ trên phố Rue de Seine ở Paris. Chủ hiệu là Arthur. Ông sáu mươi tám tuổi. Ông bán sách cũ.

Mỗi sáng, Arthur mở từng cuốn sách mới về. Ông mở chậm, từng trang một. Ông không đọc. Ông đang tìm.

Ông đang tìm những bức thư.

Người ta dùng thư làm cái đánh dấu trang. Họ quên chúng đi. Một bức thư từ mẹ. Một bức thư từ bạn. Một mẩu ghi ngắn trên mảnh giấy nhỏ. Đôi khi là một tấm ảnh. Arthur tìm thấy tất cả.

Ông đặt mỗi bức thư vào một cái hộp gỗ phía sau quầy. Hộp đã đầy. Ông làm việc này ba mươi năm rồi.

'Sao chú giữ chúng?' một cô gái trẻ hỏi ông một hôm. Cô là khách du lịch.

Arthur nói: 'Chúng không phải của tôi. Nhưng chúng không phải không có gì. Có người đã viết chúng. Có người đã đặt chúng vào sách. Tôi không thể vứt đi. Nên tôi giữ.'

'Có ai quay lại lấy không?' cô hỏi.

'Gần như không bao giờ,' ông nói. 'Hai lần trong ba mươi năm. Một người đàn ông đến lấy thư của cha. Một người phụ nữ đến lấy ảnh em gái.'

'Còn phần còn lại?'

'Chúng đợi,' Arthur nói. 'Cái hộp đợi. Tôi cũng đợi.'

Cô du khách mua một cuốn sách nhỏ. Cô không bỏ thư vào trong. Nhưng cô nghĩ về Arthur trong nhiều năm.

options: On Rue de Seine in Paris
questions.explanation: 'There is a small bookshop on Rue de Seine in Paris.'

questions.prompt: What does Arthur look for in each book?
Where does Arthur put the letters he finds?
options: In a wooden box behind the counter
In a folder under his bed
On a shelf with the books
questions.explanation: 'He puts each letter in a wooden box behind the counter.'

questions.prompt: For how long has Arthur done this?
questions.explanation: 'He has done this for thirty years.'

questions.prompt: How many people have come back for their letters?
Who came back to claim things?
options: A man for his father's letter and a woman for a photo of her sister
Two children for their grandmother's note
Two friends for the same letter
A teacher for her old class list
questions.explanation: 'A man came for his father's letter. A woman came for a photo of her sister.'

questions.prompt: Why doesn't Arthur throw the letters away?
options: Somebody wrote them and put them in a book — they are not nothing to him
He hopes to sell them one day
He uses them as bookmarks himself
questions.explanation: 'Somebody wrote them. Somebody put them in a book. I cannot throw them away.'

questions.prompt: What did the tourist do after the conversation?
options: She bought a small book and thought about Arthur for many years
She left a letter inside her book
She came back the next day
questions.explanation: 'She bought a small book. She did not put a letter inside. But she thought about Arthur for many years.'

options: Small acts of keeping — even of things that are not yours — can hold meaning for someone, someday
Used bookshops are a bad business
People should never use letters as bookmarks
Tourists in Paris always buy books
questions.explanation: The whole passage circles this idea, including 'They are not nothing.'

blanks.explanation: The shop is on Rue de Seine in Paris.
'Page by page' is a fixed phrase.
A wooden box behind the counter.
She thought about Arthur for many years.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Arthur says about the forgotten letters, 'They are not mine. But they are not nothing.' Think of one small object in your own home that is not yours but you cannot throw away. Why do you keep it — and what would change if you finally returned it to its owner, or let it go?
